attributes.stem: A 50-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with persistent back pain. This started after he injured his back four days ago when helping a friend move some furniture and he thinks he 'slipped a disc' in his lower back . The pain was 5/10 in severity at the time, and is unchanged today. He denies bowel or bladder changes, paraesthesia or fevers and has had no difficulties walking. He has been managing the pain by trying to lie flat throughout the day, occasionally attempting some gentle back stretches.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the best initial management strategy?
options: Continue with current stretching and resting
Return to normal activity with paracetamol and ibuprofen
Paracetamol and ibuprofen and a lumbar spine X-ray
attributes.explanation:
Paracetamol and codeine (Panadeine Forte) - it is inappropriate to prescribe codeine for a presentation of mild acute back pain. As per the WHO analgesic ladder, initial therapy should be simple analgesia and non-pharmacological strategies.

Ibuprofen only - while ibuprofen may be part of the management plan it would not be best practice to suggest this on its own. The patient will likely experience better pain relief with a combination of simple analgesia and non-pharmacological management. An appropriate dose of ibuprofen would be PO 8 hourly for five days and to reassess if pain was still persistent and unchanged.

Continue with current stretching and resting - it is not advisable to engage in prolonged periods of rest for mild acute pain. There do not appear to be any red flags for this patient and so it is safe for him to engage in active rest.

Return to normal activity with paracetamol and ibuprofen - this is the recommended management strategy for mild acute back pain. There are no red flags to suggest that something sinister is occurring and the mechanism of injury does not lend itself to a major trauma in an otherwise healthy individual. This would be the most appropriate initial management strategy and could be revised if he continues to experience pain. An appropriate dose of paracetamol and ibuprofen would be PO 8 hourly for five days and to reassess if pain was still persistent and unchanged.

Paracetamol and ibuprofen and a lumbar spine X-ray - it is not advised to order imaging for a presentation of mild acute back pain with no red flags. An appropriate dose of paracetamol and ibuprofen would be PO 8 hourly for five days and to reassess if pain was still persistent and unchanged.
learningPoints: Returning to normal activity with paracetamol and ibuprofen is appropriate initial management of mild acute back pain with no red flags.
references: Guideline on acute pain management. ANZCA FPM. 2013
Parr A, Askin G. Non-radicular low back pain: Assessment and evidence-based treatment. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2020;49(11):724-727.
